macro_events.event_summary: U.S.-Iran conflict escalates into a naval standoff in the Strait of Hormuz with both sides seizing ships, threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil transit.
primary_drivers: Oil supply disruption risk / Brent spike
Tanker rate spike from rerouting and war-risk insurance
Defense spending and munitions demand acceleration

beneficiaries.rationale: Majority of upstream production is non-Middle East (Permian, Guyana, deepwater) so a Brent spike flows directly to realized prices without equivalent geopolitical asset risk. Largest US-listed integrated with leveraged earnings to crude above $90.
Similar upstream leverage to Brent with minimal Iran/Hormuz production exposure; US + Kazakhstan/Australia weighted portfolio benefits from price without direct asset risk.
Pure-play Permian E&P, zero Middle East exposure, high-beta to WTI — historical correlation to oil spikes is ~2x crude move on headline days.
Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates spike hardest when Hormuz flows reroute; war-risk premiums and ton-mile demand both rise. Direct, measurable day-rate beneficiary.
Frontline VLCC/Suezmax fleet — crude tanker rates surge on Hormuz rerouting and insurance repricing; company has explicitly benefited in prior Red Sea/Hormuz incidents.
Missile defense (PAC-3, THAAD) and naval systems are the exact capabilities being consumed/deployed in a Gulf standoff; replenishment orders follow. Direct munitions demand.
Raytheon produces SM-2/SM-6 and Tomahawk used by US 5th Fleet in Hormuz engagements; standoff implies sustained munitions burn and restock contracts.
Cheniere — Hormuz disruption hits Qatari LNG exports (~20% of global LNG), directly tightening global LNG markets and boosting US export netbacks/spot cargo economics.

losers.rationale: Delta has lowest fuel hedging among legacy carriers and highest exposure to jet fuel as % of opex (~25-30%); Brent spike flows straight to cost with limited ability to pass through in near term.
Similar unhedged jet fuel exposure; also carries trans-Atlantic/Pacific routes that may require rerouting around Gulf airspace, adding block hours and fuel burn.
Carnival — bunker fuel is a top-3 cost line, and Middle East/Red Sea itinerary cancellations already pressured 2024-25; renewed conflict forces further redeployment and refunds.
FedEx Express relies on Dubai/DWC as a major hub; Gulf airspace risk plus jet fuel spike hits the highest-margin segment directly.
Consumer discretionary demand compression from a gasoline price spike (every $10 Brent ≈ $0.25/gal at pump) disproportionately hits low-income Walmart core customer spend on general merchandise.
Naphtha/ethane feedstock and energy costs rise with crude; Dow's commodity chemical margins compress when oil spikes faster than end-product pricing adjusts.

headlines: U.S.-Iran war evolves into naval standoff over Strait of Hormuz after both countries seize ships

macro_events.event_summary: Prediction market traders on Kalshi expect Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic to remain disrupted until July 2026, implying a multi-month disruption to ~20% of global seaborne oil flows.
primary_drivers: Sustained oil/LNG supply disruption through the Strait of Hormuz tightens global crude and product balances
Tanker rerouting and reduced effective fleet capacity drives shipping day rates higher
Shift in marginal barrel sourcing favors non-Gulf producers (US shale, Atlantic basin)

beneficiaries.rationale: Diamondback Energy is a pure-play Permian shale producer with ~100% US onshore exposure; sustained Brent premium from Hormuz disruption directly lifts WTI realizations and FCF with no Middle East operational risk.

Frontline's VLCC/Suezmax fleet benefits from tanker ton-mile inflation as cargoes reroute around the Cape; VLCC day rates historically spike 2-5x during Hormuz disruptions and a July normalization timeline means multiple months of elevated rates.
International Seaways operates crude and product tankers; spot-market exposure captures elevated day rates from rerouting and tight tanker supply through the disruption window.
Teekay Tankers is heavily spot-exposed in mid-size crude tankers (Suezmax/Aframax) — these see the largest ton-mile gains when Persian Gulf cargoes reroute to alternative suppliers.
Chord Energy (Bakken pure-play) benefits from sustained WTI strength; zero Middle East exposure, high oil-cut production directly levers to the premium.

Cheniere Energy benefits as European and Asian buyers scramble for non-Qatari LNG; Qatar supplies ~20% of global LNG and ships through Hormuz, making US Gulf Coast LNG the premier alternative over a multi-month disruption.
ExxonMobil's diversified upstream (Guyana, Permian, deepwater) is net-long oil price with limited Hormuz transit dependency; extended disruption supports earnings upgrades into 2Q prints.

losers.rationale: Delta lacks fuel hedges and jet fuel tracks Brent; a multi-month disruption keeping crude elevated directly compresses unit margins heading into peak summer travel.
United has high international long-haul exposure (more fuel-intensive) and minimal hedging; sustained jet fuel premium through July hits Q2 earnings directly.
Southwest's all-domestic short-haul fleet is disproportionately fuel-cost-sensitive as a % of opex; hedging program has been reduced post-2022, leaving Q2 margins exposed to extended Brent strength.
FedEx Express air network is highly jet-fuel exposed; fuel surcharge passthrough lags by 4-8 weeks, compressing margins during a multi-month disruption.

Dow Inc. uses naphtha/ethane feedstock; while US ethane is insulated, global petrochemical spreads compress when crude stays elevated and demand weakens on price. European crackers (Dow has exposure) are worst-hit.
Huntsman's polyurethanes and performance products carry high oil-linked feedstock costs with limited passthrough into weak end-markets (autos, construction); extended crude premium squeezes margins.
Carnival's bunker fuel costs are a major opex line; sustained crude strength through summer booking season hits margins and FCF guidance.

headlines: Kalshi traders don't see Strait of Hormuz traffic returning to normal until July

macro_events.event_summary: MarketWatch reports the semiconductor sector is on a historic rally, with gains broadening beyond the usual AI megacap names to less-obvious chip stocks.
primary_drivers: Sector-wide momentum and ETF/index inflows into semis
Broadening of AI capex beneficiaries beyond NVDA to second-tier names
Short-covering and rotation into previously lagging chip stocks

beneficiaries.rationale: Memory/HBM name often cited as an 'unsuspecting' AI beneficiary — not a pure AI logic play but leveraged to HBM demand. Typically featured in broadening-rally pieces as second-tier catch-up trade.
Custom-silicon/networking play that tends to be grouped with 'under-the-radar AI infrastructure' names in broadening-rally coverage; benefits from rotation out of NVDA concentration.
Analog/power semi name that has lagged the AI leaders; fits the 'unsuspecting stock' framing and benefits from generalist sector ETF flows.
Power management supplier to AI accelerators — classic 'picks and shovels' secondary beneficiary that fits the broadening narrative.
Semi cap equipment name that benefits from sector sentiment and capex cycle extension; often rallies alongside the group on broad-based semi strength.

headlines: The chip sector is on a historic tear, fueled by some unsuspecting stocks

macro_events.event_summary: US airlines are raising summer fares and cutting capacity in response to higher jet fuel costs, signaling margin pressure and disciplined supply.
primary_drivers: Higher jet fuel input costs squeezing airline margins
Capacity discipline supporting unit revenue (RASM) via fare hikes
Implied higher crude/refined product prices benefiting energy producers and refiners

beneficiaries.rationale: Southwest is structurally more fuel-hedged and domestic-focused; capacity discipline across the industry lifts its unit revenue while its simpler fleet limits exposure relative to network peers. Benefits from fare hikes sticking.
Delta's premium/loyalty mix gives it better ability to pass through fare increases; owns the Monroe refinery (Trainer) which provides a partial natural hedge against jet fuel crack spreads widening.
Valero is the largest pure-play US refiner with meaningful jet fuel/distillate output; rising jet fuel prices widen distillate crack spreads directly boosting refining margins.
Phillips 66 refining segment captures widening jet/distillate crack spreads; direct beneficiary of the same fuel price move that's hurting airlines.
Integrated major with large downstream refining and upstream crude exposure; higher crude prices implied by jet fuel jump flow to upstream earnings.

losers.rationale: JetBlue has weakest margin buffer among US carriers, minimal hedging, and leisure-heavy route network with limited pricing power — most vulnerable to fuel cost spike even with fare hikes.
Alaska has historically unhedged fuel exposure; fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs, so a jet fuel jump directly compresses EPS guidance despite capacity discipline.
Frontier's ultra-low-cost model depends on rock-bottom fares; fuel cost passthrough is hardest for ULCCs whose price-sensitive customers rebook or skip travel when fares rise.
American has the most leveraged balance sheet among legacy carriers; fuel-driven margin compression amplifies debt-service concerns and caps equity upside even with fare hikes.
Higher airfares and reduced summer capacity dampen leisure travel booking volumes; Booking's air+package and hotel attach rates face a demand-elasticity headwind.
Similar to BKNG — summer leisure travel volumes are the seasonal earnings driver, and capacity cuts + fare hikes reduce bookable inventory and trip incidence.

headlines: U.S. Airlines Raise Fares, Trim Capacity for Summer as Fuel Costs Jump

macro_events.event_summary: Italian oil major Eni announced a giant natural gas discovery offshore Indonesia, adding to a string of deepwater gas finds in the region.
primary_drivers: Incremental global natural gas supply outlook (bearish LNG prices at the margin)
Validation of deepwater SE Asia exploration economics (bullish for offshore services)
Long-dated development project pipeline requiring FPSO/subsea equipment

beneficiaries.rationale: Schlumberger has a strong footprint in Indonesian/SE Asian deepwater services and reservoir characterization; a giant gas find drives appraisal and development drilling contracts over the multi-year development cycle.
Tidewater is the largest offshore support vessel operator; incremental deepwater gas development in Indonesia tightens OSV day rates in the Asia-Pacific basin where utilization is already high.

losers.rationale: Cheniere's long-term LNG contract pricing power weakens modestly as global gas resource base expands; Indonesia is a potential competing LNG supplier to Asian buyers.

headlines: Eni Makes Giant Natural Gas Discovery Offshore Indonesia
